title: faSTM — design
text: Positioning (updated 2026-06-18). faSTM now standalone, modern successor stm, backend plugin. stm maintenance-mode (last feature release 2023, 100+ open issues), chaining hard dependency liability. faSTM dependency stm (even optional): ingests prepared text quanteda/tidytext, fits Rust, ships inspection layer (labels/FREX/coherence/exclusivity/topic correlations) honest estimateEffect. keeps emitting stm-shaped S3 object purely migration -ramp (existing scripts stm’s plot/toLDAvis keep working), runtime dependency. Text prep delegated quanteda/tidytext (don’t reinvent textProcessor). sections retain original engine/binding design, unchanged.

what: Engine & binding (unchanged from the backend design)
title: faSTM — design
text: Rust fitter (topica) returns object stm’s post-fit functions can also read — exploit migration, providing native equivalents stm never required.

what: The one architectural fact everything rests on
title: faSTM — design
text: stm’s post-fit functions — labelTopics(), plot.STM(), findThoughts(), sageLabels(), toLDAvis(), estimateEffect() — re-run estimator. pure readers fitted STM S3 object. faSTM’s stm() returns object structurally identical stm’s, entire downstream ecosystem works free, zero forking zero reimplementation. Therefore faSTM = (1) Rust fit + (2) stm-shaped object constructor + (3) pure-R estimateEffect. Text prep Imports: stm (use textProcessor() / prepDocuments() -). Plotting/labeling/LDAvis dispatch stm’s methods.

title: faSTM — design
text: user’s question : minimal package (use stm everything, faSTM estimation) vs. full fork stm backend swapped. Estimation-wins, : stm’s readers pure functions object → reproduce object shape inherit whole ecosystem, including future stm improvements. stm’s estimator one clean function: ’s R-driven EM loop C++ estep SAGE updates interleaved R. Transplanting Rust core middle loop brittle. Returning finished, compatible object . fork means owning stm’s entire codebase tracking upstream forever. thing faSTM reimplements estimateEffect, topica better stm (honest per-document posterior propagation), want anyway.

title: faSTM — design
text: topica’s STM topica::ctm::fit_ctm (STM = CTM + prevalence + content), pure Rust, public rlib: CtmModel already carries everything stm object needs: posterior_theta_samples need Rust binding. per-doc Laplace posterior fully described lambda + nu returned object, draw (η ~ N(λ_d, ν_d) → softmax → θ) pure R (MASS::mvrnorm). exactly R/posterior.R , estimateEffect() consumes. extendr crate exposes single fit_stm(...) returns CtmModel arrays R list; object assembly effects R.
code: pub fn fit_ctm<R: Rng>(     docs: &[Vec<u32>], num_topics, num_types, em_iters, em_tol, sigma_shrink,     prevalence: Option<&[Vec<f64>]>,     content:    Option<(&[usize], usize)>,   // (per-doc group id, num_groups)     init_spectral: bool, gamma_prior: GammaPrior, keep_nu: bool, diagonal: bool,     rng: &mut R, ) -> CtmModel

title: faSTM — design
text: stm documents named list 2 × n_d integer matrices (row 1 = 1-based word id, row 2 = count). topica wants docs: Vec<Vec<u32>>, doc sequence 0-based token-type ids counts expanded. Conversion (R/stm.R .Call): repeat (id-1) count times. vocab length = num_types.

title: faSTM — design
text: topica gaining opt-inference="svi" stochastic-VI path across logistic-normal family. Base CTM SVI exists today (fit_ctm_svi); STM-SVI (prevalence + content) lands #231 PR B, generalizing fit_ctm_svi. faSTM plumbs SVI kwargs day one API change needed PR B lands: inference = "batch" routes fit_ctm (today; parity-validated). inference = "svi" routes fit_ctm_svi. PR B pinned topica, svi + (prevalence|content) errors clear “requires topica >= ” message; base SVI works now. iters interpreted epochs SVI mode (topica’s convention). released faSTM pins topica revision contains STM-SVI, scaling path user needs large corpora box.

title: faSTM — design
text: Batch fit bit-reproducible (seed, vocab, documents) path carries topica’s parity validation R stm. SVI seed-reproducible (deterministic minibatch shuffle model rng), bit-exact across thread counts — documented, hidden. faSTM seeds ChaCha8Rng::seed_from_u64 mirror topica exactly (parity TODO: confirm src/python.rs STM rng construction).

title: faSTM — design
text: faSTM’s src/rust/Cargo.toml depends topica git dependency pinned tag, default-features = false (NumPy-free core; python, embeddings). Local dev uses [patch]/path override ~/Documents/GitHub/topica. eventual CRAN release, vendor cargo vendor src/rust/vendor (rextendr supports ). rand stack must match topica’s lockfile (rand 0.8, rand_chacha 0.3) Rng trait bound seed stream identical — pulling topica via git brings Cargo.lock, pin versions faSTM.

what: Build checklist before claiming “parity with stm”
title: faSTM — design
text: Map exactly slots stm reader touches; fill (half-day reading stm source). table starting point — verify plot.STM, toLDAvis, sageLabels, findThoughts real fit. Match init defaults: stm defaults spectral (init_spectral = TRUE); confirm faSTM’s default seed reproducibility. Confirm content = ~ / SAGE path reaches content_beta sageLabels() reads correctly. Recovery test: faSTM fit vs stm::stm() gadarian — topic correlation, estimateEffect coefficients, held-behavior within tolerance. SVI recovery: inference="svi" recovers inference="batch" topics within tolerance (mirrors topica’s #231 recovery gate).

title: Beyond stm: faSTM's extensions
text: stm allows single content (SAGE) covariate. faSTM accepts several crosses saturated content model: one topic-word distribution per combination levels. party (2) × chamber (2) gives 4-group content model, can read Democrats vs Republicans House vs Senate word topic differently: fit reports crossing runs, stm’s SAGE tooling works unchanged result: crossed levels stored fit (settings$covariates$contentvars, contenttable) per-covariate marginals can recovered later.
code: fitC <- stm(congress, K = 12,             prevalence = ~ party + s(congress),             content    = ~ party + chamber,    # crossed -> 4 SAGE groups             verbose = FALSE) fitC$settings$dim$A                            # number of content groups #> [1] 4 sageLabels(fitC, n = 5)          # top words per topic, per party×chamber group labelTopics(fitC, topics = 3)

title: Beyond stm: faSTM's extensions
text: Content covariates interesting partisan signal lives. , estimateEffect show Democrats Republicans devote similar prevalence budget/tax topic, word differently. ~ party content model makes concrete: shared vocabulary fiscal (tax, budget, spending), Democrats reach health, care, children, preexisting, denied Republicans reach taxing, taxed, CBO, authority: topic, opposite framing. distinction prevalence covariates can’t capture content (SAGE) covariates can.
code: fitParty <- stm(congress, K = 12, prevalence = ~ party + s(congress),                 content = ~ party, verbose = FALSE) sl  <- sageLabels(fitParty, n = 10) tax <- which(apply(sl$marginal, 1, function(w) any(grepl("^tax", w))))[1]  sl$marginal[tax, ]              # shared topic vocabulary #>  [1] "tax"        "budget"     "families"   "billion"    "health"     #>  [6] "government" "pay"        "care"       "spending"   "jobs" sl$bygroup$Democrat[tax, ]     # how Democrats word it #>  [1] "health"         "sick"           "care"           "insurance"      #>  [5] "profits"        "reconciliation" "children"       "foster"         #>  [9] "birth"          "denied" sl$bygroup$Republican[tax, ]   # how Republicans word it #>  [1] "taxing"    "harbor"    "blame"     "green"     "error"     "criticize" #>  [7] "computers" "taxed"     "extra"     "authority"

title: Beyond stm: faSTM's extensions
text: faSTM keeps stm’s method--composition uncertainty (re-drawing topic proportions per simulation) adds design features grouped data usually needs. Cluster-robust standard errors. Speeches within Congress independent, cluster congress. faSTM swaps classical vcov sandwich estimator stm’s finite-sample correction: summary() also takes p.adjust.method (e.g. "BH") correct across topics, reports per-equation R² F diagnostics. Survey weights enter way via weights = (weighted least squares per draw).
code: eff <- estimateEffect(1:12 ~ party + s(congress), fit,                       metadata = congress$meta,                       cluster = congress$meta$congress, nsims = 25) summary(eff, topics = 3) #> faSTM estimateEffect (Global uncertainty, 25 draws) #>  #> topic3: #>                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|) #> (Intercept)       0.0907     0.0045 20.0289   0.0000 #> partyRepublican   0.0046     0.0067  0.6816   0.4956 #> s(congress)1     -0.1038     0.0334 -3.1050   0.0019 #> s(congress)2      0.0663     0.0219  3.0286   0.0025 #> s(congress)3     -0.0224     0.0154 -1.4547   0.1460 #> s(congress)4      0.0326     0.0178  1.8359   0.0665 #> s(congress)5     -0.0065     0.0156 -0.4167   0.6769 #> s(congress)6      0.0203     0.0122  1.6706   0.0950 #> s(congress)7      0.0153     0.0139  1.1027   0.2703 #> s(congress)8      0.0297     0.0218  1.3633   0.1730 #> s(congress)9      0.0282     0.0257  1.0992   0.2719 #> s(congress)10    -0.0106     0.0052 -2.0476   0.0408 #>   R-squared: 0.005 | F(11,1667): 0.79

text: faSTM reads prepared text quanteda/tidytext rather tokenizing . typical preparation: vignette use bundled poliblog corpus (stm vignette’s poliblog5k), already prepared:
code: library(quanteda) dfmat <- corpus(my_data, text_field = "documents") |>   tokens(remove_punct = TRUE) |>   tokens_remove(stopwords("en")) |>   dfm() |>   dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 5) corpus <- as_corpus(dfmat)        # quanteda docvars become the metadata data(poliblog) poliblog #> <faSTM_corpus> 5000 documents, 2632 vocabulary terms, 4 metadata columns out <- list(documents = poliblog$documents, vocab = poliblog$vocab, meta = poliblog$meta)

title: Validation: parity with stm, and fit quality
text: faSTM’s optimizer stm’s, STM objective non-convex, independent run settles optimum topic numbering. question whether optimum good. fair, engine-neutral test held-predictive likelihood document completion: hold half tokens document, fit rest, score held-tokens. run held-set packages. two fits land within fraction percent held-likelihood, optima comparable quality. faSTM reaches iterations, iteration cheaper, still converges faster wall-clock time (see Get started article).
code: ho <- stm::make.heldout(out$documents, out$vocab, seed = 2138)  ff <- faSTM::stm(ho$documents, out$vocab, K = 20, prevalence = ~ rating + s(day),                  data = out$meta, init.type = "Spectral", seed = 2138, verbose = FALSE) sf <- stm::stm(ho$documents, out$vocab, K = 20, prevalence = ~ rating + s(day),                data = out$meta, init.type = "Spectral", seed = 2138, verbose = FALSE)  ll_faSTM <- mean(stm::eval.heldout(ff, ho$missing)$expected.heldout) ll_stm   <- mean(stm::eval.heldout(sf, ho$missing)$expected.heldout)  data.frame(   engine     = c("faSTM", "stm"),   heldout_LL = round(c(ll_faSTM, ll_stm), 4),   iterations = c(ff$convergence$its, length(sf$convergence$bound))) #>   engine heldout_LL iterations #> 1  faSTM    -6.9030         43 #> 2    stm    -6.8976         23 rel_gap <- abs(ll_faSTM - ll_stm) / abs(ll_stm) round(100 * rel_gap, 3)               # percent difference in held-out likelihood #> [1] 0.078 stopifnot(rel_gap < 0.02)             # within 2%

what: faSTM — a fast, modern Structural Topic Model for R
title: faSTM — a fast, modern Structural Topic Model for R
text: 📖 Documentation: https://nealcaren.github.io/faSTM/ fast, stm-compatible reimplementation Structural Topic Model R. faSTM reimplements stm’s full framework (prevalence content covariates, FREX/lift/score labels, semantic coherence, representative documents, covariate-effect estimation, model selection, --sample inference) self-contained, multithreaded Rust core, adds estimation tidy-workflow toolkit top. faSTM keeps familiar, stm-compatible API, stm analysis code runs minimal edits fitted object structurally compatible. adds top stm’s framework: estimateEffect survey weights, cluster-robust SEs, random effects, average marginal effects; multiple content covariates; NPMI/c_v coherence; broom tidiers predict() method. Replicating specific fit. STM’s objective non-convex faSTM uses optimizer, independent fit settles valid, deterministic optimum: different topic numbering, relabeling given stm run. faSTM’s spectral initialization reproduces stm’s Arora anchor-recovery step exactly, want guaranteed match can seed stm’s spectral β via stm(..., init.beta = ). four init.types supported: "Spectral", "Random", "LDA" (seeded CVB0 LDA), "Custom".
code: library(quanteda)   # tokenization (faSTM reads quanteda/tidytext objects directly) library(faSTM)  dfmat <- data_corpus_inaugural |>   tokens(remove_punct = TRUE) |>   tokens_remove(stopwords("en")) |>   tokens_wordstem() |>   dfm() |>   dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 5)  corpus <- as_corpus(dfmat)                     # docvars become metadata fit    <- stm(corpus, K = 10, prevalence = ~ Party)  label_topics(fit)                              # prob / FREX / lift / score semantic_coherence(fit); exclusivity(fit) eff <- estimateEffect(1:10 ~ Party, fit, metadata = corpus$meta) summary(eff)

title: faSTM — a fast, modern Structural Topic Model for R
text: Engine. multithreaded Rust variational EM (num_threads knob). poliblog example (5000 docs, K = 20, prevalence ~ rating + s(day)) faSTM ~6× faster stm (4s vs 23s), ~13× search_k (parallelizes across K). numbers wall-clock convergence. faSTM reaches fit iterations, one far cheaper. Scale. opt-inference = "svi" (stochastic variational) path corpora large batch EM. (Covariate-model SVI lands topica #231.) Uncertainty-aware effects. estimateEffect() uses method composition, propagating per-document posterior uncertainty, supports survey weights, cluster-robust SEs, random effects ((1 | group) via lme4), average marginal effects (ame()), combined topics. Inspection. label_topics() (prob/FREX/lift/score), topic_terms() numeric scores, coherence() (Mimno / NPMI / c_v), exclusivity(), topic_proportions(), topic correlations SAGE content labels. Multiple content covariates. content = ~ g + h fits fully crossed content model, content_topics() recovers covariate’s marginal vocabulary. Tidyverse-friendly. tidy() / glance() / augment() (broom) predict() method, alongside ggplot2 plotting layer. Self-contained. Tokenize quanteda / tidytext (field already uses) faSTM reads objects directly; runtime dependency Rust, topica, Python installed.

title: faSTM — a fast, modern Structural Topic Model for R
text: shared fit (β/θ), faSTM’s inspection numbers match stm’s: FREX/prob/lift/score labels identical stm::labelTopics, exclusivity() / semantic_coherence() match floating point. fitted object stm-shaped, stm’s readers (labelTopics(), plot.STM(), sageLabels(), findThoughts(), estimateEffect()) run faSTM fit, makes migrating existing analyses straightforward. parity given fit, claim two produce fit (see ). Validation article checks live stm: computes every inspection metric ways shared model (asserting equality stopifnot), shows faSTM’s fit, though different topic decomposition, reaches held-likelihood within fraction percent stm’s.

title: faSTM — a fast, modern Structural Topic Model for R
text: Feature-complete stabilizing toward CRAN release. Working today: Corpus ingestion quanteda / tidytext / document-term matrices. Fast fit prevalence (one several, crossed) content covariates, threaded; four init.types including real "LDA" initializer. Full inspection: labels, FREX/lift/score, coherence (Mimno/NPMI/c_v), exclusivity, topic correlations, SAGE content labels marginals. estimateEffect Global/Local/None uncertainty, survey weights, cluster-robust SEs, random effects, average marginal effects, combined topics, multiple-testing correction, R²/F diagnostics. --sample inference (fit_new_documents / predict / fitNewDocuments prior modes posterior return). Model selection (search_k, select_model, many_topics) ggplot2 plotting layer (topic summary, covariate effects CIs, search-K diagnostics, topic-correlation network + igraph export). broom tidiers, toLDAvis, topicQuality, stm-compatible exports. roadmap: stochastic variational inference covariate models (topica #231).

title: faSTM — a fast, modern Structural Topic Model for R
text: Building source compiles Rust core, needs Rust toolchain (cargo + rustc). one-time setup: install R whichever tool already (’re equivalent; devtools::install_github just calls remotes): first install compiles Rust core (fetches builds pinned, dependency-light topica-core crate GitHub, needs internet connection takes couple minutes). , package fully self-contained: runtime dependency Rust, topica, Python. Optional features pull extra R packages used: ggplot2 / ggrepel (plots), quanteda / tidytext (text prep), lme4 (random effects), glmnet (topic_lasso), igraph (topic_corr_graph), LDAvis (toLDAvis). Beta status. API stabilizing toward CRAN release. Please file issues https://github.com/nealcaren/faSTM/issues. Bug reports, rough edges, feature requests welcome.

title: Fit a structural topic model (fast Rust backend, stm-compatible object) — stm
text: documents stm-format documents: named list 2 x n_d integer matrices (row 1 = 1-based word id vocab, row 2 = count). Produced stm::prepDocuments(). vocab Character vector vocabulary terms. K Number topics. prevalence right-hand-side formula (e.g. ~ treatment + s(age)) design matrix; topic prevalence covariates. data supplies variables. content right-hand-side formula naming single categorical variable, factor; SAGE content covariate. data supplies variable. data data.frame document metadata (meta stm::prepDocuments()), aligned documents. max.em.Maximum EM iterations (batch) / epochs (svi). emtol Relative-bound convergence tolerance. init.type Topic initialization: "Spectral" (stm's default), "Random", "LDA" (seed quick CVB0 LDA, like stm's collapsed-Gibbs init), "Custom" (seed init.beta supplied model). init.beta Optional K x V topic-word probability matrix start fit given initialization (overrides init.type). Supplying R stm's exact spectral beta reproduces run — guaranteed "replicate original" mode (topica #234/#235). model fitted model whose topic-word matrix seeds init.type = "Custom". gamma.prior Prevalence-coefficient prior: "Pooled" (ridge, stm default) "L1". sigma.prior Shrinkage applied topic covariance -diagonal. seed Integer seed (batch fit reproducible ). inference "batch" (default, parity-validated) "svi" (stochastic variational; scales large corpora — requires topica build STM-SVI). batch_size, tau, kappa SVI controls (minibatch size; Robbins-Monro (tau + t)^(-kappa) step schedule). Ignored inference = "batch". num_threads Worker threads parallel variational E-step. 0 (default) uses cores; >= 1 pins scoped pool. Results identical regardless thread count. verbose Logical; print progress.

text: fast, modern Structural Topic Model R: stm-compatible fitting backend multithreaded Rust core (topica), plus effects tidy-workflow toolkit top.

title: faSTM 0.0.0.9000 (development)
text: stm(): fast STM fit via topica’s Rust fit_ctm, returning c("faSTM", "STM") object compatible stm package’s post-fit functions (labelTopics, plot, findThoughts, sageLabels, toLDAvis). Prevalence content (SAGE) covariates, spectral / LDA / random / custom initialization, num_threads knob. ~6x faster stm poliblog example (~13x search_k), wall-clock convergence. Multiple content covariates: content = ~ + b crosses covariates saturated SAGE content model (one topic-word distribution per level combination), beyond stm’s single content variable. SVI kwargs (inference, batch_size, tau, kappa) plumbed ahead topica’s STM-SVI landing (topica #231); gated prevalence/content now.

title: faSTM 0.0.0.9000 (development)
text: Effects via method composition (per-document posterior draws pooled Rubin’s rules), drawing document’s Laplace covariance, topic-estimation uncertainty propagates standard errors. Survey weights (weighted least squares) cluster-robust standard errors (sandwich vcov stm’s finite-sample correction).
